1: Agrippa said to Paul, 'You have permission to speak for yourself' (epitrepetai soi peri seautou legein): the formal grant of permission is the ritual opening of a hearing. Apologeisthai (to speak in defense) is the same verb Paul uses throughout — this is not an informal conversation but a formal apologia, the genre of self-defense speech well established in Hellenistic rhetoric (cf. Plato's Apology of Socrates). Paul stretches out his hand (ekteinas tēn cheira) — the standard orator's opening gesture, signaling the beginning of a prepared speech, not an impromptu defense.
2: 'I consider myself fortunate that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am going to make my defense today' (hēgēmai emauton makarion epi sou mellōn apologeisthai): the exordium (opening of a rhetorical speech) required a captatio benevolentiae — a gesture that wins the audience's goodwill. Paul's expression of fortune before Agrippa is formally correct: a flattering but not sycophantic opening that simultaneously identifies Paul's rhetorical competence (he knows the form) and establishes the specific ground for the compliment — Agrippa's Jewish expertise, which is the one thing Festus lacks.
3: 'You are especially familiar with all the customs and controversies of the Jews' (gnōstēn onta se pantōn tōn kata Ioudaious ethōn te kai zētēmatōn): Agrippa II's qualification as Paul's judge is his Jewish knowledge. He had been raised partly in Rome but maintained deep involvement in Jewish religious administration — he was given custody of the High Priest's vestments by Claudius, appointed high priests, and corresponded with Josephus about the accuracy of Jewish history (Life 364-366; Ant. 20.216). He was arguably the most knowledgeable person available on earth to evaluate Paul's claims about the Hebrew prophets.
4: 'My manner of life from my youth, spent from the beginning among my own nation and in Jerusalem, is known by all the Jews' (ginōskousin pantes hoi Ioudaioi): Paul's appeal to his publicly known biography is a legal tactic — he is calling on witnesses who could contradict him if false. His Jerusalem education (Acts 22:3 — under Gamaliel, in the city, trained in the law) was not private; he sat in the highest Pharisaic educational institution and was known to the Jerusalem religious establishment. The appeal to public knowledge preempts the charge that Paul is an obscure provincial with no credentials.
5: 'According to the strictest party (akribestaten hairesin) of our religion I have lived as a Pharisee' (Pharisaios): hairesis in first-century Jewish usage designated a recognizable school or party — the Pharisees, Sadducees, and Essenes are all called haireseis by Josephus (War 2.119-166; Ant. 18.11-22). Akribestaten (most precise/strict) is Paul's own assessment of Pharisaic thoroughness — they interpreted Torah more rigorously than Sadducees or the general population. Paul's Pharisaic identity is his primary credential: the most serious students of Scripture now stand before the king who knows Scripture.
6: 'Now I stand here on trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our fathers' (ep' elpidi tēs eis tous pateras hēmōn epangelias genomenes hypo tou theou): the phrase 'promise to our fathers' is patriarchal covenant language — the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 12:1-3; Acts 15:5; Acts 17:1-8), the Davidic promise (2 Samuel 7:12-16), the prophetic restoration promises (Isa 40-66; Ezek 36-37; Jer 31). Paul is reframing his trial: this is not a case about a Roman legal infraction but about whether Israel's ancestral hope has been fulfilled. He claims to stand within the stream of Israel's own expectation, not outside it.
7: 'Our twelve tribes hope to attain as they earnestly worship night and day' (to dōdekaphylon hēmōn en ekteneia nykta kai hēmeran latreuon elpizei katantēsai): the 'twelve tribes' is the ideal of all-Israel — the northern ten tribes were scattered in 722 BCE and their restoration was an eschatological hope (Isaiah 11:11-12; Ezekiel 37:15-28; Sirach Acts 36:10-11). Earnest night-and-day worship is the temple liturgy's continuous character — the daily burnt offerings, the prayer watches, the festival worship. Paul claims that what all of Israel's sincere worship aims toward — the resurrection of the dead and the fulfillment of the covenant — is what his hope concerns. The accusation that he stands against Israel is absurd: he shares its deepest longing.
8: 'Why is it thought incredible by any of you that God raises the dead?' (ti apiston krinetai par hymin ei ho theos nekrous egeirei): the rhetorical question targets the specific theological division within Judaism that made resurrection controversial. The Pharisees believed in bodily resurrection of the dead (Josephus War 2.163; Acts 23:8); the Sadducees denied it (Mark 12:18; Josephus Ant. 18.16). Agrippa presumably held the Pharisaic view. Paul's question positions the resurrection of Jesus as an instance of the general resurrection the Pharisees already affirmed; the issue is not whether God can raise the dead but whether he did so specifically in Jesus's case.
9: 'I myself was convinced that I ought to do many things in opposing the name of Jesus of Nazareth' (edoxen emautō pros to onoma Iēsou tou Nazōraiou dein polla enantia praxai): Paul's self-testimony of persecution is his most powerful credential for the reality of the conversion. The phrase 'I myself was convinced' (edoxen emautō) acknowledges the subjective certainty of his pre-conversion state — he did not merely go through the motions; he was genuinely committed to suppressing the Jesus movement. This pre-history makes the subsequent transformation inexplicable apart from the event that caused it.
10: 'I shut up many of the saints in prison and when they were put to death I cast my vote against them' (psēphon katēnenka): psēphon katēnenka (cast my vote/pebble) may indicate Sanhedric membership or authority to participate in judicial proceedings against Christians. The detail that Paul 'cast his vote' against those executed suggests participation in formal legal proceedings — consistent with the authority received from the chief priests (Acts 26:10, 12). His role was not mere bystander (contrast Acts 7:58 — consenting to Stephen's stoning) but active participant in capital proceedings.
11: 'I punished them often in all the synagogues and tried to make them blaspheme (ēnagkazon blasphēmein); and in raging fury against them I persecuted them even to foreign cities': synagogue punishment is the malkot (flogging, 39 stripes), administered for religious infractions under the authority of the synagogue's archisynagōgos and council. Forcing converts to blaspheme — presumably to recant Jesus as Lord — echoes the Roman procedure for identifying Christians (Pliny Ep. 10.96: those who invoked the gods and cursed Christ were to be dismissed). Paul had anticipated what the Romans would later formalize.
12: The Damascus journey with authorization from the chief priests: the chief priests' authority to dispatch representatives to foreign synagogues was a recognized prerogative, though its precise legal basis is debated. Damascus had a substantial Jewish population (Josephus War 2.561 — 10,500 to 18,000 Jews killed there in 66 CE; the large community implies established synagogues). The authority from the chief priests gave Paul the standing to enter Damascus synagogues and conduct interrogations of suspected Jesus-followers.
13: 'At midday (hēmeras mesēs), O king, I saw on the way a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and those who journeyed with me' (hyper tēn lamprotēta tou hēliou perilampsanta me phōs): the midday timing rules out misidentification of the sun; the light exceeds solar brightness. In the three accounts of Paul's conversion (Acts 9:3; Acts 22:6; Acts 26:13), only chapter 26 specifies the comparison with the noon sun — the superlative indicates a category different from natural phenomena. The light encompasses Paul and his companions; the encounter is not private hallucination but visible to witnesses (though the voice's full content was heard only by Paul).
14: 'I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language (tē Hebraidi dialektō), Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads (sklēron soi pros kentra laktizein)': the detail that the voice spoke Hebrew (or Aramaic — Hebraidi could indicate either) is added only in the chapter 26 account, addressing an audience familiar with Jewish tradition. 'Kick against the goads' is a Greek proverb attested in Aeschylus (Agamemnon 1624; Prometheus Bound 323) and Pindar, meaning to resist the inevitable with self-injury. Using a Greek proverb in a Hebrew speech may reflect Luke's literary shaping, or the risen Christ's willingness to meet Paul in his dual cultural context.
15: 'Who are you, Lord? And the Lord said, I am Jesus whom you are persecuting' (egō eimi Iēsous hon sy diōkeis): the self-identification formula echoes LXX divine self-disclosure — 'I am YHWH' (Exodus 3:14 LXX: egō eimi ho ōn). The identification 'whom you are persecuting' draws the equivalence that drives Paul's subsequent theology: persecuting the church = persecuting Jesus (the body-head unity of 1 Corinthians 12:12-27 and Ephesians 1:22-23). This identification is not explanatory gloss but the core revelation: the post-resurrection Jesus remains personally identified with his people.
16: 'Rise and stand upon your feet (anastēthi kai stēthi epi tous podas sou); for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and witness': the commissioning language is shaped by the OT prophet-commission pattern (Jeremiah 1:7-8; Isaiah 6:6-9; Ezekiel 2:1-3). Hypēretēn kai martyra (servant and witness) is the Lukan summary of Paul's apostolic calling — terms that appear together in Luke 1:2 (eyewitnesses and servants of the word). The appointment is specifically to be a witness 'both of the things you have seen and of things in which I will appear to you' — covering both the Damascus road encounter and subsequent revelations (cf. 2 Corinthians 12:1-4; Galatians 1:12).
17: 'Delivering you from your people and from the Gentiles — to whom I am sending you' (apostellōn se): the divine deliverance promise anticipates the repeated rescues of Acts — the Damascus escape (Acts 9:23-25), the Jerusalem rescue (Acts 9:29-30), the Philippian earthquake (Acts 16:26-34), the Corinthian protection (Acts 18:9-10), the Jerusalem riot (Acts 21:30-36), the Roman custody chain. Apostellōn (sending) is the verb behind apostolos — Paul's commission is literally an apostolic sending, with Jesus as the commissioning authority. The 'your people' and 'the Gentiles' frames the dual audience of Paul's entire missionary career.
18: 'To open their eyes (anoixai ophthalmous autōn), so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me': the commission language condenses multiple OT strands: Isaiah 42:7 (opening eyes of the blind, release from darkness — the Servant Song); Isaiah 35:5 (the blind seeing); Isaiah 61:1 (proclaiming liberty). The cosmic dualism (darkness/light, Satan/God) is characteristic of Second Temple Jewish apocalyptic (1QM, 1QS — the War Scroll's Sons of Light vs. Sons of Darkness). Paul's commission is understood within the framework of eschatological conflict between cosmic powers.
19: 'Therefore, O King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision' (tē ouranō optasią): Paul presents his subsequent life as simply obedience to a commission received. The rhetorical logic: if you accept that such a vision could occur (Acts 26:8 — you believe God raises the dead), then Paul's subsequent behavior is explained without remainder. He is not a rebel against Israel, a deviant Pharisee, or a Roman-law violator; he is a man obedient to a divine commission. The self-presentation removes moral agency from the center and places divine commission there instead.
20: 'But declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout all the region of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, performing deeds in keeping with their repentance (axios tēs metanoias erga prassontas)': the geographic sequence — Damascus, Jerusalem, Judea, then Gentiles — matches Acts' account of Paul's missionary career. The call to 'deeds worthy of repentance' echoes John the Baptist (Luke 3:8) and positions Paul's proclamation within the long tradition of ethical prophetic call. The Gentile inclusion in the repentance call is the point at which Jewish audiences regularly became hostile (Acts 22:21-22).
21: 'For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me' (ekheirazeion en tō hierō kai epeirōnto diacheiridzesthai): diacheiridzesthai (to lay violent hands on, to kill) is strong — it is the word used for the killing of Moses (Acts 7:25 implies) and other violent deaths. The seizure in the temple closes the circle of Paul's testimony: his commission was to go to Gentiles (Acts 26:17); the reaction of the temple establishment to that commission was violence. The persecution is evidence, not embarrassment: it is the prophetic pattern (Acts 7:52 — they killed those who announced the Righteous One).
22: 'To this day I have had the help that comes from God' (epikourias oun tychōn tēs apo tou theou), and so I stand here testifying both to small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would come to pass': epikourias (assistance, help) is a military term — the backup that keeps you in the fight. Paul's endurance across the gauntlet of Acts 21-26 is interpreted as divine sustaining. The limitation 'nothing beyond what Moses and the prophets said' is Paul's central apologetic claim: he has not invented a new religion but interpreted the ancient texts faithfully. The question is whether the interpretation is correct, not whether the texts exist.
23: 'That the Christ must suffer (pathētos ho Christos), and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles': pathētos ho Christos (the suffering Christ / the Christ was subject to suffering) is the key proof-text formula Paul generates from his reading of 'Moses and the prophets'. The dei (must) of suffering combined with resurrection is the interpretive claim that unlocks the whole. Isa 53 (suffering servant), Ps 22 (the righteous sufferer), Isaiah 42:6 and Acts 49:6 (light to the nations) — these are the scriptural pillars. Paul argues these texts were always pointing to a suffering-and-rising Messiah who brings light to Gentiles.
24: Festus interrupts: 'Paul, you are out of your mind! Your great learning (ta polla grammata) is driving you mad (eis manian peritrepei)!' — the charge of madness from great learning is a stock trope in Hellenistic intellectual culture: Socrates was accused of corrupting youth through too much philosophy; the Cynics were widely regarded as mad. Festus's category is the Roman administrator's: a man clearly educated (the speech has been sophisticated) who has lost the distinction between religion and reality. The accusation is culturally calibrated — it is the polite Roman way to dismiss claims that violate the categories of empirical governance.
25: 'I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus (kratiste Phēste), but I am speaking true and rational words' (alētheias kai sōphrosynēs rhēmata apophthengomai): sōphrosynēs (soundness of mind, self-control, reasonableness) is the exact antithesis of mania — Festus's charge and Paul's defense are using the same philosophical vocabulary. Sōphrosynē was one of the cardinal virtues in Hellenistic ethics; Paul is claiming not merely sanity but philosophical virtue. The appeal to 'true' (alētheias) words alongside sōphrosynē frames his proclamation as both factually accurate and philosophically coherent.
26: 'The king knows about these things (epistamai gar autos toutōn ti ho basileus proslalon autō) and to him I speak freely (parrēsiazomenos), for I am persuaded that none of these things has escaped his notice, for this has not been done in a corner' (ou gar estin en gōnia pepragmenon touto): the appeal to Agrippa's knowledge is tactically brilliant. Paul shifts the rhetorical ground from defending himself to inviting Agrippa into his own knowledge-network. The phrase 'not done in a corner' asserts the public character of Jesus's ministry and the resurrection witness — it is not an esoteric private claim but events with public witnesses in Jerusalem (Jesus crucified under Pilate; the tomb; the appearances).
27: 'King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe' (pisteueis... oida hoti pisteueis): the direct question to Agrippa is Paul's rhetorical turn — he moves from defendant to questioner, from addressing the bench to engaging Agrippa personally. The double assertion (do you believe? I know you do) puts Agrippa in an impossible position: to say 'no' is to alienate his Jewish constituency; to say 'yes' is to acknowledge the force of Paul's scriptural argument. The question is the culmination of the entire speech: if you accept the prophets, and the prophets described a suffering-rising-Gentile-illuminating Messiah, then the question of Jesus's identity cannot be dismissed as madness.
28: Agrippa's response: 'In a short time (en oligō) would you persuade me to be a Christian (Christianon poiēsai)?' — the precise meaning of en oligō is debated: 'in a little time/with little effort' vs. 'almost'. The RSV renders 'Almost you persuade me to be a Christian'; the ESV 'In a short time you would persuade me.' Either way, Agrippa deflects with humor or irony rather than engagement — a courtly deflection. The use of 'Christian' (Christianos) is the third occurrence in Acts (Acts 11:26 — Antioch; Acts 26:28; cf. 1 Peter 4:16) — an external label, here used to describe what Paul is trying to make Agrippa become.
29: Paul's response: 'Whether short or long, I would to God that not only you but also all who hear me this day might become such as I am — except for these chains (ektos tōn desmōn toutōn)': the gesture toward his chains is the speech's most powerful moment — the chained prisoner wishing his accusers might share everything he has except the chains. The exemption of the chains from the wish is not self-pity but realism; the substance of what Paul wishes — the apostolic calling, the direct encounter with the risen Christ, the commission to both peoples — is what he commends. The rhetorical effect is to reverse the roles: the powerful are the impoverished; the prisoner is the one with everything worth having.
30: The king rose, and the governor and Bernice and those sitting with them (anestē te ho basileus kai ho hēgemōn hē te Bernikē kai hoi synkathēmenoi autois): the rising of the assembled dignitaries signals the formal end of the hearing. The departure in sequence — king, governor, then the rest — follows protocol. Luke's scene-construction gives maximum theater: the elaborate entrance of Acts 26:23 (great pomp, tribunes, prominent men) is balanced by this formal exit. What happens in the corridor is the ironic verdict.
31: 'This man is doing nothing to deserve death or imprisonment' (ouden thanatou ē desmōn axion ti prassein ton anthrōpon touton): the verdict exchanged among the departing officials is the fifth Roman declaration of Paul's innocence in Acts (Lysias Acts 23:29; Festus Acts 25:25; now Agrippa). The pattern has been building across chapters 22-26; every competent authority who examines the case reaches the same conclusion. Luke's point is legal and theological simultaneously: the Roman empire, operating under its own procedural standards, repeatedly declares the gospel-bearer innocent.
32: 'This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar' (apeluhthai edynato ho anthrōpos houtos ei mē epekeklēto Kaisara): Agrippa's final assessment closes the Roman judicial sequence and pivots to the final movement of Acts. The appeal to Caesar has made Paul's Rome journey legally required — it cannot be revoked. What appears as a procedural constraint is, for Luke, the providential mechanism by which the divine plan (Acts 23:11 — 'you must testify also at Rome') is fulfilled. Paul appealed because he needed to get to Rome; Rome is where he needed to be. Roman law serves divine purpose.
